document.docAuthor: Kataev, Boris M., Hongbin, Liang & Wrase, David W.
document.docTitle: Trichotichnus anthracinus Ldn., B. O. Landin 1955
document.masterDocTitle: New data on carabid beetles of Trichotichnus s. str. (Coleoptera: Carabidae) of Yunnan (China) and adjacent areas, with description of six new species and two new subspecies

mods:titleInfo.mods:title: New data on carabid beetles of Trichotichnus s. str. (Coleoptera: Carabidae) of Yunnan (China) and adjacent areas, with description of six new species and two new subspecies

mods:name.mods:affiliation: Zoological Institute, Russian Academy of Sciences, Universitetskaya nab. 1, St. Petersburg 199034, Russia.

Key laboratory of Zoological Systematics and Evolution, Institute of Zoology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100101, China. lianghb @ ioz. ac. cn; https: // orcid. org / 0000 - 0002 - 9668 - 1167

paragraph.materialsCitation:
Holotype of
T. anthracinus
: female, “N. E. Burma Kambaiti ,
7000 ft
,
4–8/6 1934
, R. Malaise ”, “Typus”, “
Trichotichnus anthracinus Ldn., B. O. Landin
det.”, “
Trichotichnus (s. str.) anthracinus Landin Det. N. Ito 1994
”, “NHRS-JLKB 000041167”, “5692 E91 +”, “Naturhistoriska Riksmuseet Stockholm Loan no 461/93” ( NRM ).

Re-description ( 5 males and 5 females measured). Body length 7.2–7.7 mm . Habitus as in Figs. 1 & 2 .

caption.paragraph:
FIGURES 1–2.
Trichotichnus anthracinus Landin, 1955
, habitus (1, Yunnan; 2, Myanmar, holotype). Scale bar = 1.0 mm.

subSubSection.paragraph: Body black, shiny on dorsum. Labrum, base of mandibles, palpi, antennae, tibiae and tarsi brownish yellow to brown; femora blackish brown to black.

Head with large and convex eyes (HWmax/PWmax 0.66–0.70, HWmin/PWmax 0.53–0.58, HWmax/HWmin 1.19–1.29). Genae about as wide as antennomere 2 basally. Fronto-ocular furrows distinct, reaching supraorbital furrows. Left mandible sharply bent in the apical quarter, blunted at tip. Mentum and submentum not fused. Ligular sclerite markedly widened apically. Dorsal microsculpture obliterated, consisting of very fine, more or less isodiametric meshes.

Pronotum (PWmax/PL 1.49–1.58), widest before the middle (PWmax/PWmin 1.16–1.26), more strongly narrowed apically than basally, with base slightly longer than apex and with sides almost straightly converging in basal half. Apical margin very shallowly emarginate, basal margin almost straight, both completely bordered. Lateral bead complete, very slightly widened basally. Basal angle obtusangular, sharp at apex, usually with very small obtuse denticle. Disc moderately convex, flattened at basal angles, densely distinctly punctate along base and along sides, occasionally with finer and sparse punctures at apical margin; basal foveae oval, shallow. Microsculpture consisting of obliterated transverse meshes.

Elytra (EL/EW 1.41–1.50, EL/PL 2.88–3.04, EW/PWmax 1.27–1.35) convex, oval, widest in the apical third, with prominent rounded humeri and very shallow preapical sinuations; sutural angle acutangular, sharp or slightly blunted at tip. Internal humeral angle very obtuse. Striae slightly impressed along entire length. Intervals somewhat flat, slightly convex apically. Parascutellar (abbreviate) striole moderately long, with a setigerous pore at base. Interval 3 with a small discal setigerous pore adjacent to stria 2 before the apical third. Marginal umbilicate series more or less continuous or interrupted at the middle. Microsculpture consisting of very fine transverse lines.
Prosternum with very fine short setae. Metepisternum markedly longer than wide, strongly narrowed posteriorly.

Legs relatively short. Protibia on dorsal side without longitudinal sulcus, on outer margin preapically with one stouter and two very thin spines in male and with three comparatively stout spines in female. Tarsi glabrous dorsally, pro- and mesotarsomeres 1–4 in both sexes with comparatively sparse ventro-lateral setae. Metatarsus shorter than HWmax, with tarsomere 1 about as long as tarsomeres 2 and 3 combined. In male, protarsomeres 2 and 3 not elongate, about as long as wide or slightly wider than long, respectively; mesotarsomere 1 shorter than mesotarsomeres 2 and 3 combined, with adhesive scales apically.

caption.paragraph:
FIGURES 3–6.
Trichotichnus anthracinus Landin, 1955
(Yunnan). 3, 4, median lobe of aedeagus; 5, gonocoxite; 6, laterotergite, gonosubcoxite, and gonocoxite. Lateral (3 & 5), dorsal (4) and ventral (6) views. Scale bars: A = 0.5 mm (5 & 6), B = 1.0 mm (3 & 4).

subSubSection.paragraph:
Comparison. Among the Chinese species of the nominotypical subgenus,
T. anthracinus
is most similar in size and habitus to
T. vicinus (Tschitschérine, 1897)
and
T. miser (Tschitschérine, 1897)
. The latter two species easily differ from it in having femora not infuscate, last visible abdominal sternite ( VII ) in male with two pairs of marginal setae and median lobe of aedeagus in lateral view not bent dorsally at apex.
Trichotichnus miser
can also be distinguished from
T. anthracinus
by more obtuse, almost rounded basal angles of pronotum.

Distribution ( Fig. 7 ). Northeastern India , northeastern Myanmar and South West China (northwestern Yunnan ) ( Kataev & Wrase 2017b ). According to the examined material, the species occurs at altitudes of 800–3400 m ; in Yunnan , it is most common at altitudes of 2000–3000 m .

Remarks.
Trichotichnus anthracinus
was described from one female ( Fig. 2 ) collected in Kambaiti (northeastern Myanmar ), very close to Yunnan Province ( China ).
Trichotichnus ellipticus
was based on one male from Meili in Hengduan Mountains (northwestern Yunnan ). Their synonymy was stated on examination of the holotype of
T. anthracinus
and comparison with the original description of
T. ellipticus
and with additional material from Myanmar and Yunnan ( Kataev & Wrase 2017a: 18 ). In the original description ( Ito 2014 ),
T. ellipticus
was compared only with
T. fukuharai Habu, 1957
.
